text: The surgeon evaluates the rectal prolapse, assessing its length and size along with the functional status of the anal sphincter complex. A submucosal infiltration of adrenaline is applied to minimize bleeding during dissection. A circular incision is made in the rectal wall using monopolar diathermy, isolating the rectum and leaving the mesorectum visible to partially fill the pre-sacral space.
During the anterior dissection of the rectum, the surgeon takes particular care to avoid damaging the posterior wall of the vagina, which is clearly visible with the opening of the Douglas pouch.

Following the isolation and detachment of the rectum, the surgeon applies gentle traction to the proximal colon to evaluate the redundant bowel. A sealing instrument is used for rapid pericolonic dissection. The surgeon performs a posterior pelvic floor repair with non-absorbable stitches to reinforce the area. The redundant colon is resected, and an end-to-end coloanal anastomosis is completed using interrupted non-absorbable stitches.
The surgeon performs a final check to assess the integrity of the coloanal anastomosis, adding additional stitches to fill any visible gaps.
